title: QCommE2E: An Open-Source Simulation of End-to-End Quantum Communication Systems
abstract: This paper presents QCommE2E as an open-source simulation framework for end-to-end quantum communication systems, with explicit tutorial emphasis. The primary objective is to develop a comprehensive framework that includes transmitters, receivers, communication channels, performance metrics, and visualization tools, to facilitate the systematic design, configuration, and analysis of experimental simulations for novel quantum communication architectures. As the primary use case, we walk through the current quantum channel comparison, which maps textbook quantum-information channels and reduced optical-fiber/free-space surrogates into a single executable benchmark. We describe the common density-matrix interface, the matched modulation and detection chain, and the exact role of the channel classes Depolarizing Channel, Dephasing Channel, Erasure Channel, Bosonic Channel, Turbulence Channel, and PMD Channel. We also explain the current visualization layer, which projects received states onto constellation and Bloch representations for qualitative inspection. To keep the implementation-faithful, we provide a summary of the baseline execution, which uses a square 16-QAM embedding, a pretty-good-measurement detector constructed from the same reference-state codebook, and BER/SER. Finally, we position the channel-comparison as an entry point for broader future work, including equalization, quantum autoencoder, learning-based, and system-level algorithm integration.
summary: This paper introduces QCommE2E, an open-source simulation framework designed for modeling end-to-end quantum communication systems. It provides a unified way to simulate various quantum channels and evaluate performance metrics like BER and SER. The framework is particularly useful for designing and benchmarking novel quantum communication architectures.
main_problem: The lack of a comprehensive, open-source, and tutorial-oriented simulation framework capable of integrating heterogeneous quantum channels (qubit-based and bosonic surrogates) for end-to-end quantum communication system analysis.
main_result: The development of QCommE2E, a modular Python-based simulation framework that successfully demonstrates a unified pipeline for comparing various quantum channel degradation mechanisms using 16-QAM modulation.
method: A modular end-to-end pipeline (Modulator -> Transmitter -> Channel -> Receiver -> Metrics) using a density-matrix interface and a Pretty Good Measurement (PGM) detector.
model_or_system: A quantum communication link utilizing density-matrix representations of quantum states, incorporating channels such as Depolarizing, Dephasing, Erasure, Bosonic (truncated), Turbulence, and PMD surrogates.
key_observables: Bit Error Rate (BER), Symbol Error Rate (SER), constellation diagrams, and Bloch sphere representations.
important_parameters: 16-QAM embedding, 4000 symbol benchmark, 3dB bosonic loss, and various channel-specific noise parameters.
main_assumptions: The 16-QAM embedding is an engineering approximation rather than a full physical model; the bosonic channel is restricted to a low-dimensional (dim=2) subspace; the system is assumed to be memoryless.
figures_summary: Figures 1 and 2 illustrate the impact of weak turbulence on 16-QAM constellations and Bloch spheres, while Figures 3 and 4 show the effects of 3dB loss in a bosonic channel.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the software motivation, details the mathematical interface and channel implementations, demonstrates a benchmark channel-comparison workflow, and concludes with future work directions.
why_interesting: It provides a unified software tool for simulating open quantum systems and noise models (CPTP maps) in the context of quantum communication, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and network design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Constellation diagram for the weak-turbulence configuration. Left: transmitted 16-QAM surrogate constellation. Right: corresponding received constellation.
Fig. 3. Constellation diagram for the bosonic channel with 3dB loss configura- tion. Left: transmitted 16-QAM surrogate constellation. Right: corresponding received constellation.
Fig. 2. Block spheres for the weak-turbulence configuration. Left: transmitted 16-QAM surrogate constellation. Right: corresponding received constellation.
Fig. 4. Block spheres for the bosonic channel with 3dB loss configuration. Left: transmitted 16-QAM surrogate constellation. Right: corresponding re- ceived constellation.
